executive_summary: WIN-066 contains a CRITICAL citation error: the DOI 10.1038/ngeo2516 resolves to Doetterl et al. (2015) 'Soil carbon storage controlled by interactions between geochemistry and climate' — a paper about soil carbon, not hemispheric energy balance. This is a fabricated citation that would immediately discredit our review if a dome defender checked it. Additionally, the +0.34 W/m² figure is mischaracterized: recent literature (Raghuraman et al., 2025, PNAS) identifies 0.34 ± 0.23 W/m²/decade as an EMERGING TREND in absorbed solar radiation asymmetry (linked to aerosol changes, ice melt, and cloud shifts), not a static hemispheric difference caused by land-ocean distribution. Ironically, Stephens et al.'s actual research emphasizes hemispheric near-SYMMETRY in Earth's energy balance — the opposite of what our review implies. The verdict ('Std Model Explains') is directionally correct but the supporting evidence is fabricated and the scientific framing is wrong. Major rewrite required.

dome_defense_attempt_1.title: The +0.34 W/m² is a real measurement, and our review's citation is fake
dome_defense_attempt_1.argument: A dome defender who checks the DOI would immediately discover it links to a soil carbon paper, not an energy balance paper. They could claim: 'The critical review fabricates citations to appear authoritative. If they'll make up a DOI for this WIN, what else is fabricated?' This ad hominem would be devastating because it's factually correct — the DOI IS wrong.
dome_defense_attempt_1.our_counterargument: This is our most embarrassing error on WIN-066. The DOI must be corrected immediately. The correct reference for hemispheric energy asymmetry would be Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015, 'The Earth's energy balance', Surv. Geophys., doi:10.1007/s10712-015-9340-1) or Loeb et al. (2018, 'Clouds and the Earth's Radiant Energy System', J. Climate). The +0.34 W/m²/decade trend comes from Raghuraman et al. (2025, PNAS, doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122). We need the right paper with the right DOI.

dome_defense_attempt_2.title: The dome IS geometrically asymmetric and predicts hemispheric energy differences
dome_defense_attempt_2.argument: On the dome's flat disc, the North Pole is at the center and the 'southern hemisphere' is the outer rim. The sun orbits at 5,733 km altitude in a circle whose radius varies seasonally (smaller in NH summer, larger in NH winter). This geometry necessarily delivers different solar energy to the inner disc (NH) vs outer disc (SH). The dome DOES have geometric reasons for hemispheric energy asymmetry — the critic just didn't check.
dome_defense_attempt_2.our_counterargument: The dome has no published heat-transport equations, albedo model, or radiation budget calculation. Having an asymmetric geometry is not the same as deriving +0.34 W/m² from that geometry. The dome would need to show: (1) solar flux as a function of disc radius, (2) integration over the annual solar orbit, (3) comparison of inner vs outer disc totals. None of this exists on the dome site. Without a derivation, claiming 'the dome predicts this' is post-hoc adoption of an observation. However, our review SHOULD acknowledge that the dome's geometry is inherently asymmetric, and explain why geometric asymmetry alone doesn't constitute a prediction.

dome_defense_attempt_3.title: Stephens et al. actually found hemispheric SYMMETRY, undermining the standard explanation
dome_defense_attempt_3.argument: A knowledgeable dome defender could point out that Stephens et al.'s famous result is that Earth's hemispheres are remarkably SYMMETRIC in their energy balance (reflected shortwave nearly identical despite very different surface types). This is the OPPOSITE of what our review claims. The dome defender could argue: 'Your own cited paper says the hemispheres are balanced, yet you claim the asymmetry is a well-understood standard physics result. You're contradicting your own source.'
dome_defense_attempt_3.our_counterargument: This is a legitimate attack. Stephens' work shows that the TOA radiation budget is nearly hemispherically symmetric — a surprising result given the different land-ocean fractions. The asymmetry the dome claims (+0.34 W/m²) is actually an emerging TREND identified in recent CERES data (2000-2024), not a static equilibrium feature. Our review conflates two different things: (1) the well-known land-ocean distribution difference, and (2) the recently-detected trend in hemispheric energy absorption. These have different causes and different magnitudes. We need to be more precise about what's being claimed and what explains it.

dome_defense_attempt_4.title: Standard physics hasn't fully explained the asymmetry trend — the dome fills the gap
dome_defense_attempt_4.argument: The dome could argue that the emerging hemispheric asymmetry (PNAS 2025) is not yet fully attributed. While researchers point to aerosol changes, ice melt, and cloud shifts, significant uncertainty remains (0.34 ± 0.23 W/m²/decade — the error bar is 68% of the signal). If standard physics can't fully explain it, maybe dome geometry provides the missing mechanism.
dome_defense_attempt_4.our_counterargument: This is the 'god of the gaps' argument applied to climate science. The fact that attribution is still being refined does not mean any alternative model automatically fills the gap. The dome would need to: (1) derive a specific energy asymmetry from its geometry, (2) show that the predicted trend matches observations better than standard models, (3) explain WHY the trend is emerging now (since dome geometry is static). The dome does none of this. However, our review should acknowledge that the +0.34 W/m²/decade is an active research area, not a settled number. Oversimplifying weakens our credibility.

code_analysis_validation.monitoring_notes: No monitor.py or pull_data.py files exist in the dome-model-review repository. No monitoring code references WIN-066, 'heat', 'NH', 'asymmetry', or domain 66. This WIN has zero automated validation. The dome's repository source code does not appear to contain any climate data monitoring scripts.
code_analysis_validation.relabels_standard_notes: The hemispheric energy asymmetry is studied in standard climate science via CERES satellite data, energy budget analyses, and GCMs. The dome model's contribution is claiming this asymmetry 'confirms dome asymmetry' without changing any numerical prediction or providing a distinct mechanism. This is vocabulary substitution: standard physics → dome geometry.
code_analysis_validation.post_hoc_notes: The hemispheric energy budget has been studied since at least the 1990s (Trenberth & Fasullo). Stephens et al. (2015) and subsequent CERES analyses quantify the asymmetry. The dome model's V51.0 (2026) claims this as a WIN after decades of published observations. The +0.34 W/m² figure is adopted from published literature, not predicted beforehand.
code_analysis_validation.derives_from_dome_notes: No formula on the dome site derives +0.34 W/m² (or any hemispheric energy asymmetry) from dome parameters. The dome geometry (disc_radius=20,015 km, sun_altitude=5,733 km) could in principle create asymmetric solar flux, but no such calculation is presented. The value is adopted from standard observations without derivation.

relevant_files: raw-text/ directory (no monitor.py or pull_data.py found)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists for WIN-066. The dome's repository contains no climate energy budget scripts, no CERES data fetching, and no hemispheric comparison logic.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no validation exists. The WIN appears to be a manually-added claim with no automated checking.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None identified for this specific WIN. The dome does not invoke aetheric refraction or other escape mechanisms for energy budget claims.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No code claims are made about WIN-066 in our review (code_analysis is null). The absence of monitoring code is consistent with the pattern: WIN-066 would be tagged 'none' for monitoring.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Searched entire raw-text/ directory and dome-model-review repository. No monitor.py, pull_data.py, or any Python script references WIN-066, hemispheric energy, heat asymmetry, or CERES data. Zero monitoring infrastructure for this WIN.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Hemispheric energy asymmetry is quantified by Stephens et al. (2015), Loeb et al. (2018), Raghuraman et al. (2025) using CERES satellite data and climate models. The dome claims the same observation as 'confirming dome asymmetry' without providing a different numerical prediction or distinct mechanism.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Hemispheric energy budget studies predate the dome model by decades (Trenberth 1997, Stephens 2012, 2015). The +0.34 W/m² trend was published in PNAS 2025. Dome V51.0 released March 2026 — well after all relevant observations.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome formula derives hemispheric energy asymmetry from dome parameters (disc_radius, H(r), sun_altitude). The dome's geometry is asymmetric (center vs rim), but no radiation budget calculation translates this geometry into a W/m² prediction. The value is adopted wholesale from standard observations.

holes_found.description: FABRICATED DOI: The citation 'Stephens et al. (2015, Nature Geoscience)' with DOI 10.1038/ngeo2516 resolves to Doetterl et al. (2015) 'Soil carbon storage controlled by interactions between geochemistry and climate' — a paper about soil carbon, not hemispheric energy balance. A dome defender checking this DOI would immediately discredit our entire review.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace DOI immediately. The correct reference for hemispheric energy balance is likely Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015, 'The Earth's energy balance', Surv. Geophys.) or Stephens et al. (2012, Nat. Geosci., doi:10.1038/ngeo1580). For the +0.34 W/m²/decade trend specifically, cite Raghuraman et al. (2025, PNAS, doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122).

holes_found.description: MISCHARACTERIZED FIGURE: The +0.34 W/m² appears to be a TREND (per decade) from recent CERES data (Raghuraman et al., 2025 PNAS), not a static hemispheric difference. Our review and the dome both present it as if it's a fixed asymmetry. The distinction between a static equilibrium feature and an emerging trend matters: a static feature could be structural (land-ocean), but a trend implies temporal change (aerosols, ice melt, clouds).
holes_found.recommendation: Clarify what +0.34 W/m² actually represents. If it's the PNAS trend figure, note it's per decade and attributable to aerosol changes + Arctic ice loss + cloud shifts. If the dome cites a different +0.34 value, trace its actual source.

holes_found.description: WRONG SCIENTIFIC FRAMING: Stephens et al.'s famous result is that Earth's hemispheres are remarkably SYMMETRIC in energy balance (a surprising finding). Our review says 'well-studied consequence' as if the asymmetry is established fact, when the key Stephens finding is about symmetry. A dome defender familiar with this literature could use this to argue we don't understand our own sources.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe the evidence. Acknowledge that the TOA energy balance is remarkably symmetric (per Stephens), and that the +0.34 W/m² is either: (a) a small residual within the near-symmetric budget, or (b) a recently emerging trend. Either way, the dome provides no mechanism, but our characterization must be scientifically accurate.

holes_found.description: MISSING DOME GEOMETRY ACKNOWLEDGMENT: The dome IS geometrically asymmetric (NP at center, sun orbiting at varying radii). Our review says 'dome provides no mechanism for hemispheric heat differences' without acknowledging the dome's inherent geometric asymmetry. A defender could argue we're ignoring the obvious asymmetric geometry.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence: 'While the dome's disc geometry is inherently asymmetric (NP at center, varying firmament height), no radiation budget calculation on the dome site translates this geometry into a specific W/m² prediction. Geometric asymmetry is not a quantitative prediction.'

holes_found.description: LAND FRACTION IMPRECISION: Our review states '39% land vs 19% in the south.' Standard references give NH land fraction as ~39.3% and SH as ~19.1%. These are approximately correct, but the '2× more land' in the finding oversimplifies — it's the difference in thermal inertia, albedo, and evaporation that matters, not just area ratio.
holes_found.recommendation: Minor — the values are close enough. But the finding could be improved: 'Caused by NH having ~2× more land area (different heat capacity and albedo); dome has no heat-transport mechanism.'

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's static geometry cannot explain a TREND. If the +0.34 W/m² is an emerging decadal trend (PNAS 2025), it implies temporal change. The dome's geometry is fixed — it cannot predict why the asymmetry is growing over time. Standard physics explains the trend via declining NH aerosols, Arctic ice loss, and cloud changes. The dome has no mechanism for any of these temporal drivers.

The dome's own sun geometry predicts the WRONG direction of asymmetry for half the year. In the dome model, the sun orbits at larger radius (more energy spread over larger area) during SH 'summer' and smaller radius (concentrated energy) during NH summer. This would predict the NH gets MORE intense but BRIEFER solar exposure — the net effect depends on the integral. The dome has not computed this integral, making its 'prediction' meaningless.
sources: Dome site: sun_altitude=5,733 km, disc_radius=20,015 km

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model identifies a real observation: there IS a measurable hemispheric asymmetry in Earth's energy budget, and it is an active area of climate research. The dome is also correct that its geometry is inherently asymmetric, which in principle could produce differential solar heating. These are genuine insights about the physical world.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Having an asymmetric geometry is not the same as predicting a specific asymmetry. The dome provides no formula, no radiation integral, and no numerical derivation connecting its geometry to +0.34 W/m². The observation was published decades before V51.0, making this post-hoc adoption. And the dome's static geometry cannot explain why the asymmetry is an emerging trend, which standard physics attributes to aerosol reductions, ice loss, and cloud changes — phenomena the dome model has no framework to address.

deeper_analysis: WIN-066 is a textbook example of the dome model's 'observation adoption' pattern: take a real, published measurement, claim it 'confirms dome asymmetry,' and provide no derivation. The verdict ('Std Model Explains') is correct in direction — standard physics does explain hemispheric energy differences through land-ocean distribution, albedo, and circulation patterns. However, our review has three serious problems: (1) a fabricated DOI that would destroy credibility if checked, (2) mischaracterization of +0.34 W/m² as a static difference when it may be a decadal trend, and (3) oversimplification of the science by ignoring that Stephens et al. found remarkable hemispheric SYMMETRY, not asymmetry. These errors are especially damaging because WIN-066 is one of the EASIER WINs to rebut — the dome provides zero derivation and zero monitoring. Our sloppiness here is unforced. The fix is straightforward: correct the DOI, clarify the science, and add the geometric asymmetry acknowledgment.

citations_failed.citation: Stephens et al. (2015, Nature Geoscience) doi:10.1038/ngeo2516
citations_failed.detail: DOI 10.1038/ngeo2516 resolves to Doetterl et al. (2015) 'Soil carbon storage controlled by interactions between geochemistry and climate' (Nature Geoscience 8, 780-783). This is a paper about SOIL CARBON, not hemispheric energy balance. The DOI is fabricated/incorrect.
citations_failed.correct_reference: For Earth's energy balance: Stephens et al. (2012, Nat. Geosci., doi:10.1038/ngeo1580) 'An update on Earth's energy balance in light of the latest global observations'; or Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015, Surv. Geophys., doi:10.1007/s10712-015-9340-1). For the +0.34 W/m²/decade trend: Raghuraman et al. (2025, PNAS, doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122).
